source.title: Irreversible and Catastrophic
source.venue: Cornell Law Review, vol. 91, no. 4, pp. 841-897

confidence_rationale: Sunstein's article is the canonical legal-philosophy treatment of how to define and act under 'irreversibility' when the agent does not have certainty about whether harm is irreversible. He distinguishes two operational conceptions (option-value and qualitative-distinctiveness) and proposes the Irreversible Harm Precautionary Principle, which is exactly the kind of principled-without-certainty answer Q-027 is asking for. Source quality is high (peer-reviewed flagship law review by a leading regulatory theorist). Mapping fidelity is moderate -- the framework is built for environmental and public-health regulation, not for a computational agent's harm signal -- and transfer risk is real because REE has no equivalent of the regulatory option-purchasing market. The paper supports the existence of such a definition without giving REE one it can directly adopt.
failure_signatures: Sunstein argues that 'irreversibility' has no single coherent definition and offers two distinct operational conceptions (option-value vs. qualitative-distinctive loss), so any REE definition that picks one will inherit specific weaknesses of that conception.

Sunstein explicitly notes that 'irreversible harms are sometimes on all sides of social problems', meaning a precautionary rule that always favours preserving optionality can itself foreclose options. A naive translation to REE would risk freezing the agent on both sides of any uncertainty-laden harm choice.

The Irreversible Harm Precautionary Principle works by 'purchasing an option to prevent the harm at a later date'. REE has no obvious analogue for purchasing options against future foreclosure -- the architectural translation is non-trivial and the paper does not provide it.

mapping.source_claim_statement: When a harm is potentially irreversible and regulators lack information about its magnitude and likelihood, the right principle is to 'purchase an option' to prevent the harm at a later date (the Irreversible Harm Precautionary Principle). 'Irreversibility' itself can be understood either as the loss of option-value (the future flexibility foreclosed by an action) or as a qualitatively distinctive class of loss that cannot be repaired by later compensation.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-027 asks whether REE can define irreversible harm without requiring certainty that a harm is irreversible. Sunstein's option-value conception suggests one principled answer: REE could treat a harm as irreversible-enough-for-precaution when it would foreclose future trajectories that the agent currently cannot reproduce -- i.e., when the resulting state has measurably reduced reachability across the agent's plausible future state-space. This makes irreversibility a property of foreclosed reachability rather than a property of physical processes. Sunstein's qualitatively-distinctive conception suggests a complementary answer: certain harm classes (those affecting INV-026 self-existence, INV-029 love-bearing capacities) carry a categorical weight that should not be traded against routine harms. Together these provide a structural template: REE's harm signal could carry a separable irreversibility component computed from foreclosed-reachability + categorical-class membership, neither of which requires certainty.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Sunstein writes for human regulators choosing among policies in an institutional context. REE is a single computational agent. The option-value framing translates only if REE has a meaningful sense of 'future trajectories foreclosed', which is non-trivial in a partially-observable environment. The qualitatively-distinctive framing risks importing human moral category-distinctions that REE has no axiomatic basis to draw. The paper supports the form of an answer to Q-027 but does not give REE a directly implementable definition.
mapping.source_context: Legal-philosophy review article in a major US law journal. Empirical examples drawn from environmental policy (climate change, endangered species), public health (mad cow), and national security (terrorism).

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Cornell Law Review, leading author). Mapping fidelity moderate -- the option-value framing has a real structural analogue in REE (foreclosed reachability) but needs work to instantiate; qualitative-distinctiveness is more interpretive. Transfer risk moderate because the institutional regulatory context does not transfer to a single agent's internal valuation.
